topic: A tenant receives an eviction notice
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp white paper felt like sandpaper under Clara’s fingertips. She reread the bolded section three times, the ink blurring slightly as her gaze darted to the signature line. Every shadow in the hallway seemed to deepen, stretching the familiar furniture into menacing shapes. A cold knot tightened low in her stomach, a familiar, unwelcome clenching that made her jaw ache. She stood by the window, watching the neighbor’s cat saunter across the lawn, her breath catching in shallow, rapid bursts. The silence of the apartment was suddenly deafening, amplifying the frantic thumping of her own pulse against her ribs. She needed to find something, anything, to ground herself before the sheer weight of the document threatened to pull her under.

[story 2]

Mark smoothed the notice flat on the worn kitchen table, his hand trembling just enough to make the cheap laminate vibrate. He kept wiping his palms on his jeans, a nervous habit he hadn't managed to shake. The fluorescent light above the sink hummed with an irritating, high-pitched whine that grated on his nerves. He kept glancing toward the front door, half-expecting someone to materialize, someone to deliver the news in person. His throat felt dry, constricted. *This can’t be happening,* he thought, the conviction warring fiercely with a rising tide of dread. He picked up a chipped mug, the ceramic feeling strangely hot and unstable in his grip, and focused intently on the pattern of the cracks in the glaze, trying to anchor his scattered thoughts.

[story 3]

When the mail slot rattled, Leo snatched the envelope with a jerky movement, his knuckles white against the thick cardstock. He didn't look at the contents immediately; instead, he focused on the way his shoulders were hiked up toward his ears, a physical manifestation of internal tension. The air in the small living room seemed suddenly too thin to draw into his lungs. He paced three steps toward the bookshelf, then stopped abruptly, his eyes fixed on the framed photograph on the mantelpiece, refusing to acknowledge the stark reality of the notice lying in his hand. A low, involuntary sound escaped his chest—a small, sharp intake of breath—as he fought to maintain a semblance of composure, his gaze darting around the room as if searching for an escape route that didn't exist.
